title: Learning to Look, Learning to Ask

suggested.title: [Look Together] A hand lens or magnifying glass

supplementary.title: A thermometer, a rain gauge, and a simple weather station

competence_markers.description: The student can observe and describe a natural object or phenomenon with specific detail, using multiple senses, before offering an interpretation
The student maintains a nature journal with regular, dated entries that include drawings and written descriptions
The student can design a simple fair test — identifying what they are changing, what they are measuring, and what they are keeping the same
The student can carry out a simple experiment and accurately report what happened, even when the result was unexpected
The student can articulate the difference between "I think" and "I tested" and give an example from their own experience
The student notices and records patterns in nature and asks questions about why those patterns occur
The student demonstrates comfort with being wrong — they treat unexpected results as interesting rather than as failure
The student engages with narration prompts with specific detail from their own observations and experiments, not generic statements

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me about something you observed in your nature journal that surprised you.
narration_prompts.detail: What did you expect to see? What did you actually see? What was different?

narration_prompts.prompt: Describe an experiment you did. What question were you trying to answer?
narration_prompts.detail: What did you do to test it? What happened? Was the test fair? How do you know?

narration_prompts.prompt: What is the difference between what you think and what you have tested?
narration_prompts.detail: Give me an example from your own experience.

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me about a pattern you noticed in nature.
narration_prompts.detail: What is the pattern? How many times did you observe it? Why do you think it happens?

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me about Galileo and what he discovered.
narration_prompts.detail: What did he test? What did he find? What happened to him? Why is his story important?
If I wanted to test whether red apples or green apples are heavier, how would I set up a fair test? What would I need to keep the same?

narration_prompts.prompt: Pick something you observed this week and describe it using as many senses as you can.
narration_prompts.detail: What did you see? Hear? Feel? Smell? What details did you notice that most people would miss?

cross_connections.description: The observation skills developed here are applied directly to physical phenomena — forces, motion, states of matter. The student who has learned to observe carefully is ready to observe physical laws in action.
Kitchen experiments in this packet introduce the student to chemical changes (bread rising, browning) that are formalized in 1.6.3.

Nature journaling and organism study developed here are the direct foundation for biological observation in 1.6.4. The student arrives with an established habit of careful observation of living things.
Measurement in experiments (length, temperature, time) reinforces the practical numeracy developed in 1.5.1. Science and math are natural partners.
Nature journaling builds writing fluency. The student who describes observations in words is practicing the same skills developed in 3.2.1.
The garden-as-laboratory approach in this packet connects directly to the observational practices in 2.5.1.
The discipline of regular observation, journal-keeping, and sustained multi-week experiments reinforces the habit architecture established in IC1.1.

content.why_this_matters:
You are going to learn how to find things out for yourself.

Most people, when they want to know something about the world — why the sky is blue, why ice melts, why a ball rolls downhill — ask someone else or look it up. There is nothing wrong with asking or looking things up. But there is something better: learning to find out for yourself, by looking carefully, asking good questions, and testing your ideas to see if they are right.

This is called science, and it is not something that only happens in laboratories with people in white coats. Science is something you can do anywhere — in your yard, in your kitchen, on a walk, at a pond. Science starts with one simple habit: **paying attention to what is actually happening, rather than what you expect to happen.** That turns out to be much harder than it sounds, and much more interesting than you might think.

In this packet, you will practice the most important skill in all of science: observation. You will learn to look at the world carefully, to notice things other people miss, to ask questions about what you notice, and to test your questions with simple experiments. You will keep a nature journal where you record what you see. And you will begin to understand the difference between what you think and what you have tested — because those are not the same thing, even though most people treat them as if they are.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Observation as a discipline** — the habit of looking carefully before speaking, noticing before explaining, recording before interpreting
2. **The question-test-observe cycle** — the foundational rhythm of all scientific inquiry, practiced hundreds of times until it is second nature
3. **Fair testing** — the concept that to learn anything from a test, you must keep everything the same except the one thing you are testing
4. **Nature journaling** — the practice of regular, careful recording of what is observed in the physical world
5. **The distinction between opinion and evidence** — the understanding that "I think" and "I tested" are both valuable starting points, but they are fundamentally different things
6. **Pattern recognition** — the habit of noticing when the same thing happens repeatedly in nature, and asking why
content.core_content:
### Part 1: What Do You Notice?

Science begins with a question that sounds simple but is not: **What do you notice?**

Not "What do you think?" Not "What do you already know?" Not "What did the book say?" Just: What do you notice — right now, with your own eyes, ears, hands, and nose?

This is harder than it sounds. Most people — children and adults — look at something and immediately start explaining it. They see a bird and say "That bird is looking for food." Maybe it is. But what did you actually see? A bird on the ground. Hopping. Tilting its head. Pulling something from the soil. Those are observations — things you actually saw. "It is looking for food" is an interpretation — a guess about what the observation means.

Both observations and interpretations are valuable. But they are different things, and a scientist always knows which one they are doing.

**Practice this everywhere:**
- Look out the window. What do you notice? Not what you think — what you see, hear, feel.
- Go outside. Sit in one place for five minutes. What do you notice that you did not see in the first thirty seconds?
- Look at a leaf. Describe it. Not what kind of leaf it is — what it looks like. The shape, the color, the texture, the veins, the edges.

The goal is to slow down your eyes and your mind. To look before you explain. This is the first and most important skill in science, and you will practice it throughout this entire packet.

### Part 2: The Nature Journal

Starting today, you will keep a nature journal.

A nature journal is a personal record of what you observe in the natural world. It is not a diary. It is not a school assignment. It is a scientist's notebook — a place where you record what you see, when you see it, and what it looked like.

**What goes in the nature journal:**
- **Drawings.** Draw what you see. You do not need to be a good artist. You need to look carefully enough to draw what is actually there. Drawing forces you to notice details you would miss if you only wrote words. Draw the leaf. Draw the insect. Draw the clouds. Draw the bird. Label the parts you notice.
- **Words.** Describe what you observed. Where were you? What time was it? What was the weather like? What did you see, hear, smell, feel?
- **Questions.** Every time you observe something and wonder about it — "Why does this leaf have spots?" "Why do the ants walk in a line?" "Why is this rock a different color than that one?" — write the question down. You do not need to answer it yet. Just write it down. Questions are valuable.
- **Dates.** Always date your entries. Over time, you will start to notice patterns — the same birds appear at the same time of year, the same plants bloom in the same sequence, the temperature follows a pattern. Dates make these patterns visible.

**How often:** At least twice a week, and ideally more. The best nature journaling happens when it becomes a habit — something you do regularly, like eating breakfast. It does not need to take long. Ten minutes of careful observation and recording is better than an hour of unfocused wandering.

### Part 3: Questions Can Be Tested

Some questions can be answered by looking. "What color is the bark of that tree?" Look at it and you know.

Some questions can be answered by reading. "How far away is the moon?" Someone has measured it, and you can look up the answer.

But some questions can be answered by **testing** — by doing something and seeing what happens. These are the most interesting questions, and they are the heart of science.

Here is a question that can be tested: **Does a ball roll farther on carpet or on a wooden floor?**

You might already think you know the answer. But thinking you know and having tested it are different things. To test it, you would:
1. Get a ball.
2. Roll it on carpet. Measure how far it goes.
3. Roll it on a wooden floor with the same amount of push. Measure how far it goes.
4. Do it several times to make sure.
5. Now you know — not because you guessed, but because you tested.

That is the question-test-observe cycle, and it is the foundation of everything scientists do. Ask a question. Think of a way to test it. Do the test. Observe what happens. The result is knowledge — not opinion, not guess, but something you found out for yourself.

### Part 4: What Makes a Fair Test?

There is a problem with testing. If you are not careful, your test will not tell you what you think it tells you.

Suppose you want to test whether plants grow better in sunlight or in shade. You put one plant on the windowsill and one in a closet. The windowsill plant grows bigger. Does that prove sunlight helps plants grow?

Maybe. But what if the windowsill plant was also in a bigger pot? Or got more water? Or was a different kind of plant? If anything else was different besides the sunlight, you cannot be sure that sunlight was the reason.

A **fair test** is a test where you change only one thing and keep everything else the same. The one thing you change is the thing you are testing. Everything else stays the same so that you know the result was caused by the thing you changed and not by something else.

In the plant example, a fair test means:
- Same kind of plant
- Same size pot
- Same amount of soil
- Same amount of water
- Same temperature
- The only difference: one gets sunlight, one does not

This is one of the most important ideas in all of science. If your test is not fair, your result does not mean what you think it means. Scientists spend enormous effort making sure their tests are fair — and even professional scientists sometimes get it wrong.

### Part 5: "I Think" Versus "I Tested"

There is nothing wrong with thinking. Having ideas, guessing, imagining what might be true — these are wonderful and important. Every scientific discovery started with someone thinking, "I wonder if..."

But **thinking something is true and knowing something is true are different.** An idea — even a very good idea — is not the same as evidence.

Here is the difference:
- "I think heavy things fall faster than light things." That is an idea. It seems reasonable. Many people throughout history believed it.
- "I dropped a heavy ball and a light ball from the same height at the same time, and they hit the ground at the same time. I did it ten times and it happened every time." That is evidence. And the evidence says the idea was wrong.

The man who first tested this carefully was Galileo Galilei, and he lived over 400 years ago. Before Galileo, people had believed for over a thousand years that heavier things fall faster — because it seemed obvious, and nobody bothered to actually test it. Galileo tested it. The evidence said otherwise. That is how science works: you test your ideas against reality, and if reality disagrees, you change your ideas.

This does not mean your ideas are bad. It means you are learning. Every scientist in history has been wrong many times. Being wrong and finding out you are wrong is not failure — it is the process. The only real failure in science is refusing to test your ideas, or refusing to accept the results when you do.

### Part 6: Patterns in Nature

As you observe the world carefully — in your nature journal, on your walks, in your experiments — you will start to notice something: **patterns.**

The sun rises in the same direction every morning. Water always flows downhill. Seeds always grow roots downward and shoots upward. Birds of the same kind sing the same song. Certain plants bloom at the same time every year. Puddles always evaporate on sunny days and not on cold ones.

These patterns are not accidents. They are clues. When you notice a pattern, you are noticing something about how the world works. And when you notice a pattern, you can start to ask: **Why does this happen?**

Why does water always flow downhill? (Because of gravity — you will learn about this in 1.6.2.)
Why do seeds grow roots down and shoots up? (Because the plant can sense gravity and light.)
Why do puddles evaporate faster on hot days? (Because heat gives water molecules enough energy to escape into the air.)

You do not need to know these answers yet. You just need to notice the patterns. The explanations will come later. For now, the practice is: **observe, record, notice patterns, ask questions.** That is the rhythm of science, and you are already doing it.

### Part 7: The Five Senses as Scientific Instruments

Your body is the first and most important scientific instrument you will ever own.

**Eyes:** Look at color, shape, size, movement, texture, shadow, reflection. How big is it? What shape? Does it move? How does the light hit it?

**Ears:** What sounds do you hear? Are they high or low? Loud or soft? Constant or changing? Where are they coming from? The bird song in the morning is different from the bird song in the evening. What is different about it?

**Touch:** (When safe to touch.) Is it rough or smooth? Hard or soft? Warm or cold? Wet or dry? Heavy or light? Flexible or rigid?

**Smell:** What does it smell like? Sweet? Sharp? Earthy? Does the soil smell different after rain? (It does — that smell has a name: petrichor, and it is caused by bacteria in the soil.)

**Taste:** (Only when the mentor says it is safe.) Taste is chemistry — your tongue detects molecules. But you should never taste anything in nature without your mentor's approval, because some things that look harmless are not.

Using all your senses together gives you more information than any one sense alone. A good scientist uses every tool available — and your senses are your first tools.

### Part 8: Weather Watching

Weather is the most accessible daily science laboratory you have. It changes every day, it is always happening, and it is observable without any special equipment.

**Start a weather log.** Every day, at roughly the same time, record:
- Temperature (if you have a thermometer) or a general description (cold, cool, warm, hot)
- Sky (clear, partly cloudy, overcast, stormy)
- Wind (calm, breezy, windy, very windy; from what direction)
- Precipitation (none, light rain, heavy rain, snow, etc.)

After a few weeks, patterns will emerge. You will notice that certain cloud shapes tend to come before rain. You will notice that temperature changes in a rhythm. You will notice that wind from a certain direction often brings a particular kind of weather.

This is real meteorological observation — the same basic practice that weather scientists have used for centuries. You are building a dataset with your own hands and eyes.

### Part 9: Animals and Plants as Subjects of Study

The living things around you — in your garden, in your yard, along your walking path, even in your house — are subjects of scientific study.

**Choose one organism and observe it over time.**

If you choose a plant:
- How tall is it? Measure it. Come back in a week and measure again.
- When does it bloom? When do the blooms fade?
- What insects visit it? At what time of day?
- What does it need? What happens if it does not get water? (Observe, do not kill it.)

If you choose an animal (a bird, an insect colony, a squirrel):
- Where does it go? When?
- What does it eat? How does it get its food?
- Does it behave differently at different times of day?
- What happens when it encounters another animal?

Record all of this in your nature journal. Over weeks and months, you will build a detailed picture of one living thing — a picture that no one else in the world has, because no one else has watched this particular organism as carefully as you have.

### Part 10: When Scientists Were Wrong

Here is something important: scientists are wrong all the time. And that is fine. Being wrong is part of how science works.

For a very long time, people believed that the Earth was the center of the universe — that the sun, the moon, and all the stars moved around the Earth. This seemed obvious. After all, you can see the sun moving across the sky. Important, respected people believed this. They taught it in schools for over a thousand years.

Then a man named Copernicus said: what if the Earth moves around the sun instead? People thought he was wrong. But over time, as more careful observations were made — by Galileo, by Kepler, by others — the evidence was clear. The Earth does move around the sun. The old idea was wrong.

This did not mean the people who believed the old idea were stupid. They were using the best evidence they had. But science is not about being right the first time. It is about being willing to change your mind when better evidence comes along.

This is true for you too. Sometimes you will test an idea and find out it was wrong. That is not failure. That is discovery. You learned something you did not know before — you learned that your idea did not match reality. That is valuable.

### Part 11: Science Is Not Just for Scientists

You do not need a laboratory, a white coat, or a university degree to do science. You need your eyes, your brain, and the habit of asking questions and testing them.

A farmer who notices that certain crops grow better in certain soil and tests different methods is doing science. A cook who experiments with recipes — changing one ingredient and observing the result — is doing science. A person who notices that they feel better when they sleep more, and tests this carefully over several weeks, is doing science.

Science is a way of thinking, not a profession. It is the habit of paying attention, asking questions, testing ideas, and being honest about what you find. You can use it anywhere, on anything, for the rest of your life.
content.formative_practices:
### Study 1: The Observation Walk [Ongoing — Weekly or More]
Go outside. Walk slowly. Stop often. At each stop, the student practices the "What do you notice?" discipline — describing what they observe with all available senses before offering any interpretation. The mentor asks follow-up questions: "What else?" "Look closer." "What does it sound like?" After the walk, the student makes a nature journal entry about their best observation of the day.

### Study 2: The First Experiment [Early in Packet]
Choose a simple testable question from the student's own curiosity — or from the suggestions in *Outdoor Science Lab for Kids*. The student designs the test with mentor guidance, making sure it is a fair test. Carry out the experiment. Record the results. Discuss: did the result match what you expected? If not, what did you learn?

### Study 3: The Fair Test Challenge [Mid-Packet]
Present the student with a series of simple experiments and ask them to identify whether each is a fair test. "I tested whether sugar dissolves faster in hot water or cold water. I put a spoonful of sugar in a cup of hot water and two spoonfuls in a cup of cold water." Is that fair? Why not? What would you change? The student practices spotting unfair tests and redesigning them.

### Study 4: The Galileo Story [Mid-Packet]
Tell the student about Galileo — how he tested whether heavy things fall faster than light things, how he used a telescope to see things no one had seen before, and how he was punished for saying the Earth moves around the sun. Discuss: why is it important to test ideas even if everyone believes something else? Has the student ever been sure about something and then found out they were wrong?

### Study 5: The Plant Growth Experiment [Multi-Week]
Design and carry out a simple plant growth experiment: Does a plant grow better in sunlight or shade? In wet soil or dry soil? With music playing or in silence? The student chooses the question, designs a fair test, plants the seeds, observes daily, and records growth over several weeks. This is the packet's longest experiment and teaches the discipline of sustained observation over time.

### Study 6: Organism Study [Multi-Week]
The student chooses one organism — a tree, a bird species, an ant colony, a garden spider — and observes it over several weeks, making regular nature journal entries. At the end, the student narrates everything they learned about this organism from direct observation. What does it do? When? What does it seem to need? What patterns did you notice?

### Study 7: Pattern Collection [Ongoing]
The student maintains a running list of patterns they have noticed in nature. Each pattern gets a journal entry: what the pattern is, when they noticed it, and their best guess about why it happens. Review the list periodically and discuss: have any of these guesses been tested? Could they be tested? How?

### Study 8: The Kitchen Experiment [Late Packet]
Conduct a simple experiment in the kitchen — does bread rise faster in a warm place or a cold place? Does salt dissolve faster in hot water or cold water? Does an apple turn brown faster when cut open or when left whole? The student designs the fair test, conducts it, and records the results. This establishes that science happens at home, not just outside.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Observation Quality
**Developing well:** The student notices specific details — color, texture, shape, behavior, change over time. Descriptions are concrete and sensory. The student looks before speaking and often says "I didn't notice that before."

**Developing slowly:** The student gives vague or generic descriptions. "It's a tree." "The bird flew away." No specific detail, no sensory richness. Observations are replaced by explanations or by statements of prior knowledge.

**Diagnostic questions:** Are you asking "What do you notice?" consistently? Are you giving enough time for the student to look before you move on? Are you modeling your own careful observation? Young students may need smaller, more focused subjects — one leaf rather than a whole tree.

### 2. Nature Journal Practice
**Developing well:** The journal has regular entries with dates. Drawings show genuine observation (the leaf has the right number of points, the insect has the right number of legs). Written descriptions include specific detail. Questions are recorded.

**Developing slowly:** The journal is sparse, entries are undated, drawings are generic or copied from memory rather than observed, writing is minimal. The student resists journaling.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the journal accessible and part of the routine? Is the student trying to make it "perfect" and therefore resistant? (If so, emphasize that messy, quick sketches are great.) Is outdoor time happening regularly?

### 3. Fair Test Understanding
**Developing well:** The student can explain what makes a test fair — change one thing, keep everything else the same. Can identify when a test is unfair and suggest how to fix it. Applies the concept naturally when designing experiments.

**Developing slowly:** The student designs experiments that change multiple things at once. Cannot identify what should be kept the same. Does not see why fairness matters.

**Diagnostic questions:** Have you done enough physical experiments for the concept to be concrete? The fair test concept is best learned by doing many experiments, not by being told the rule. If the student does not grasp it abstractly, do more experiments and point out the fairness issue each time.

### 4. Comfort with Being Wrong
**Developing well:** When an experiment produces an unexpected result, the student is interested rather than upset. "That's not what I expected!" is said with curiosity. The student does not try to change the result or pretend it matched their prediction.

**Developing slowly:** The student is frustrated or embarrassed when their prediction is wrong. They may try to alter the result, insist they meant something different, or lose interest in the experiment.

**Diagnostic questions:** Are you modeling comfort with being wrong? When your own prediction does not match the result, do you say so cheerfully? The student learns the emotional posture of science from watching you.

### 5. Question Generation
**Developing well:** The student regularly asks "why" and "what if" about the physical world. Questions are specific and often testable. The student has accumulated a list of questions in their nature journal.

**Developing slowly:** The student rarely asks questions about the physical world. Observations do not lead to curiosity. Questions are generic or unrelated to what was observed.

**Diagnostic questions:** Are you spending enough time in environments that provoke curiosity? A walk through a forest, a visit to a pond, time in a garden — these generate more questions than sitting indoors. Are you protecting the student's questions from dismissal? Every question should be received as valuable.
mentor_guide.common_pitfalls:
### Answering Questions Too Quickly
When the student asks "Why do the leaves change color?" the temptation is to explain chlorophyll. Resist this. Instead: "That's a great question. What do you think?" or "Let's watch and see what we notice over the next few weeks." The goal is not to deliver correct answers. The goal is to develop the habit of inquiry. An answer given too quickly short-circuits the process.

This does not mean you never answer questions. It means you let the student wonder first, hypothesize first, and observe first. Then, when the explanation comes — from you, from a book, from their own investigation — it has a place to land.

### Replacing Observation with Information
It is tempting to turn nature walks into lectures — to point at a bird and say "That's a robin, it migrates south in the winter, it eats worms and insects." This is information delivery, not scientific observation. The student learns to listen rather than to look.

Instead: "Look at that bird. What do you notice about it?" Let the student observe. Then, if appropriate, add information: "That's called a robin. I wonder why it's pulling at the ground like that." Information should follow observation, not replace it.

### Making Experiments Too Complicated
The best experiments for this age are extremely simple. Does the ball roll farther on carpet or on wood? Does ice melt faster in the sun or the shade? Does the paper airplane fly farther when thrown hard or soft? Complexity kills interest at this age. Keep it simple, keep it physical, keep it fast enough to hold attention.

### Neglecting the Journal
If the nature journal becomes an afterthought — something done occasionally when remembered — the most important practice in this packet is being lost. Build it into the routine. Make it non-negotiable but short. Even five minutes of drawing and writing after an observation walk is sufficient.
mentor_guide.narration_evaluation:
### Prompt 1: Surprising Observation
**Strong:** Describes a specific observation with concrete detail. Explains what they expected versus what they actually saw. Shows the ability to distinguish expectation from observation.
**Weak:** Vague or generic. "I saw a bug." No detail, no surprise, no contrast between expectation and reality.

### Prompt 2: Experiment Description
**Strong:** Names a specific question, describes the test procedure, reports the result accurately, and can say whether the test was fair. Includes concrete details about what they did.
**Weak:** Cannot describe the experiment clearly. Confuses the question with the result. Cannot articulate what they did or why.

### Prompt 3: "I Think" vs. "I Tested"
**Strong:** Gives a clear, specific example from their own experience where they had an idea, tested it, and found out whether it was right. Understands that the test is what made the difference.
**Weak:** Cannot distinguish between thinking and testing. Treats personal belief as equivalent to evidence.

### Prompt 4: Nature Pattern
**Strong:** Describes a specific, real pattern they observed over time. Has observed it multiple times. Offers a reasonable guess about why it happens and knows that the guess has not yet been tested.
**Weak:** Names a pattern from a book rather than from observation. Cannot describe specific instances.

### Prompt 5: Galileo
**Strong:** Retells the story with specific detail — what Galileo tested, what he found, what happened to him. Connects it to the broader idea that testing matters even when everyone believes something else.
**Weak:** Vague summary. Cannot name what Galileo actually tested or why his story matters.

### Prompt 6: Fair Testing
**Strong:** Can design a fair test for the given scenario. Identifies what to change, what to measure, and what to keep the same. Spots unfairness in flawed scenarios.
**Weak:** Cannot identify what needs to stay the same. Designs tests that change multiple things at once.
